subject: LU-10193 osd-ldiskfs: backup index object with plain format

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Patch Set 10: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 10: Code-Review+1

my major concern is still same here.. given we have to scan everything (except /O and /ROOT) to find all the indices all this stuff (register indices, track that runtime, etc) makes no sense, instead we should scan/backup when it's requested.
I think this is a serious technical debt.

Patch Set 10:

> my major concern is still same here.. given we have to scan everything (except /O and /ROOT) > to find all the indices all this stuff (register indices, track that runtime, etc) makes no sense,
> instead we should scan/backup when it's requested.
> I think this is a serious technical debt.

Scanning the non-ROOT and non-O when mount server is NOT new logic for this patch, instead, it is the existing initial OI scrub logic. Here, we just check whether the object is index or not during the initial OI scrub, comparing with the existing initial OI scrub itself, such check overhead is almost nothing.

Patch Set 10:

> it is the existing initial OI scrub logic. 

so it shouldn't be an issue to run the same logic at umount when backup is requested and don't do any kind of registereing/checking stuff runtime.

not to say that scanning at every mount is not a good idea generally (given number of objects  grow over time).

Patch Set 10:

> so it shouldn't be an issue to run the same logic at umount when backup is requested and don't do any kind of registereing/checking stuff runtime.

Scanning the index objects when umount is not impossible, it may not bring too much benefit to us. But introducing new scanning when umount conditionally. Since the initial OI scrub is already there, from the implementation, sharing the existing scan mechanism may be better and easier.

Patch Set 10:

>  it may not bring too much benefit to us

the benefit is good and obvious - to remove register/unregister stuff adding additional and _not needed_ code. and additional runtime code which is not needed 99.(9)% of time.

Patch Set 10:

As we discussed before, there is not un-register process in current implementation, that was the most concerned issue in old patch.

Patch Set 10:

> there is not un-register process in current implementation
this is not that much better as you still to register it, which is almost never needed.
that isn't just cycles, it's the code others will need to take care of

Patch Set 10:

It is true that for most of  cases, the registered index objects may be useless if not required backup. But the details have been hidden inside OSD, the index users do not need care about that.
Patch Set 10: Code-Review+2
Change has been successfully cherry-picked as a48853cf3dc51b24cc276fcf5f2e3e25a32c0d25 by Oleg Drokin

parents.subject: LU-10193 osd-zfs: backup index object with plain format

commit.subject: LU-10193 osd-ldiskfs: backup index object with plain format
commit.message:
LU-10193 osd-ldiskfs: backup index object with plain format

This patch is mainly for migrating filesyste between ZFS
backend and ldiskfs backend via server file level backup
and restore. It will dumps the ldiskfs special formatted
index object to the local '/index_backup' directory with
the name of source index's FID string and ".lbx" postfix
when umount device.

The format of the backup is as following (same ZFS case):
1) header: 512 bytes, including:
   magic:       4 bytes
   count:       4 bytes
   keysize:     4 bytes
   recsize:     4 bytes
   owner_fid:   16 bytes
   padding:     480 bytes

2) body: after the header, <key, rec> pairs one by one.

The backup will be done when server umount. The backup behavior
is controlled via new OSD lproc interface "index_backup". It is
off by default. You can turn it on to enable backup when server
umount via writing non-zero value to such lproc interface.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: I5ac81dd470f3cb29eb3c9ec0e01935c9b1a0fda9
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/30911
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
